file_name: Korfiati et al. - 2021 - ebioMelDB Multi-modal database for melanoma and its application on estimating patient prognosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper applied machine learning methods to obtain results in melanoma prognosis. Specifically, it used an ensemble dimensionality reduction technique employing a multi-objective optimization heuristic algorithm to select feature subsets and optimal parameters for classifiers including linear SVM, Radial Basis Function Kernel SVM, and Random Forest. These models were used to predict the vital status of cutaneous melanoma patients based on RNA-Seq gene expression data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the creation of the 'ebioMelDB' database and the application of machine learning algorithms (ensemble dimensionality reduction with SVM and Random Forest) to predict patient prognosis using RNA-Seq data. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity) from 5-fold cross-validation experiments and compares them with state-of-the-art methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: ebioMelDB: Multi-modal Database for Melanoma and Its Application on Estimating Patient Prognosis
journal: 17th IFIP International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Applications and Innovations (AIAI)
brief_summary: This study introduces ebioMelDB, a multi-modal database integrating RNA-Seq gene expression, clinical data, and dermoscopy images for cutaneous melanoma. The authors applied an ensemble dimensionality reduction technique using a multi-objective optimization heuristic to select feature subsets and optimal parameters for classifiers including linear SVM, RBF SVM, and Random Forest. These models were used to predict the vital status of melanoma patients from TCGA data across different time windows. Evaluation via 5-fold cross-validation showed accuracy rates of 92.46% for 5-year, 97.80% for 3-year, and 100.00% for 1-year predictions. The results significantly outperformed other implementations of Support Vector Machines and Random Forests, as well as state-of-the-art algorithms reported in recent literature. The database is made available to support future research in melanoma diagnosis, treatment response, and prognosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; used in prognosis prediction (vital status) and as a diagnostic category in the ebioMelDB database.
Benign category in the ebioMelDB database; includes common and atypical nevi from datasets like PH2 and ISIC.
Benign category in the ebioMelDB database grouping various non-nevus benign lesions from ISIC 2019/2020 and other datasets.
Benign category in the ebioMelDB database for lesions that are benign but have suspicious features, such as actinic keratosis (AK) and atypical melanocytic proliferation.
Malignant category in the ebioMelDB database grouping non-melanoma skin cancers like BCC and SCC.

all_task_types.target: Vital status (alive vs dead) of cutaneous melanoma patients at specific time windows (total follow-up, 5-year, 3-year, and 1-year).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly treats the prediction of patient vital status as a two-class classification problem. The models (Linear SVM, RBF SVM, Random Forest) predict discrete labels ('alive' or 'dead') based on RNA-Seq gene expression data. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity via 5-fold cross-validation. While the authors mention plans to apply a regression version to compare with other studies, the actual work reported in this paper is classification.

datasets_used.name: ebioMelDB (Image Collection)
datasets_used.description: A curated multi-modal database integrating diverse public datasets of clinical and dermoscopy images for melanoma and other skin lesions. It merges naming conventions from multiple sources into broader categories.

use.other: The database was constructed and curated to host the widest collection of clinical and dermoscopy images organized in categories. It is made available for future research, diagnosis, and prognosis studies, but was not used for training the specific prognostic models in this study.

datasets_used.name: 7-PT Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset provided by Kawara et al. used for 7-point melanoma checklist criteria classification and skin lesion diagnosis, containing dermoscopic and clinical images.

use.other: Included in the ebioMelDB image collection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset for dermoscopic image classification among nine different diagnostic categories.

use.other: Included in the ebioMelDB image collection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset for dermoscopic image classification tasks of benign and malignant skin lesions.

use.other: Included in the ebioMelDB image collection.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE Dataset
datasets_used.description: A non-dermoscopic image dataset from Giotis et al. on the computer-assisted diagnostic system MED-NODE.

use.other: Included in the ebioMelDB image collection.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset containing images for common/atypical nevi and melanoma.

use.other: Included in the ebioMelDB image collection.

datasets_used.name: TCGA-SKCM (via GDC Data Portal)
datasets_used.description: The Cancer Genome Atlas Skin Cutaneous Melanoma project data downloaded from the NIH GDC Data Portal. Contains RNA-Seq gene expression quantification (HTSeq-Counts) and clinical data for melanoma patients.

use.training: Used for training the machine learning classifiers (Linear SVM, RBF SVM, Random Forest) to predict vital status.

datasets_used.name: NCBI GEO (Gene Expression Omnibus)
datasets_used.description: A public repository for high-throughput microarray and next-generation sequencing functional genomic datasets. The study curated 178 series from GEO containing melanoma RNA-Seq data.

use.other: Used to build the biological data component of the ebioMelDB database.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify statistically significant differentially expressed genes between patient groups (alive vs dead).
To correct p-values for multiple testing during the identification of differentially expressed genes.
To evaluate the performance of machine learning classifiers (Linear SVM, RBF SVM, Random Forest) in predicting patient vital status across different time windows.

To perform ensemble dimensionality reduction, selecting optimal feature subsets and classifier parameters by minimizing selected features while maximizing accuracy, F1 score, F2 score, precision, recall, and ROC_AUC.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in gene expression levels between the patient groups (alive vs dead).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in gene expression levels between the patient groups (alive vs dead).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The adjusted p-value threshold was set to 0.01 to identify 611 statistically significant differentially expressed genes after correcting for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg FDR adjustment method.

Proposed Method (Ensemble Dimensionality.Classification Models: 4 RF models and rbf SVM models

TCGA-SKCM.notes: The proposed method clearly outperformed other methods.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble dimensionality reduction technique employing a multi-objective optimization heuristic algorithm (incorporated in InSyBio Biomarkers tool) selecting from Linear SVM, Radial Basis Function Kernel SVM, and Random Forest
architecture_used.input_size: 471 samples with 60488 features (genes), narrowed to 11339 genes, then to 414, 13, 163, or 7 selected features depending on the time window

architecture_details.method_type: Ensemble dimensionality reduction with multi-objective optimization
architecture_details.classifier_selection: Best classifier among linear SVM, RBF SVM, and Random Forest selected by the heuristic
architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Final prediction made by the majority of the classifiers among multiple equally performing models

classification_or_output_head.decision_mechanism: Majority voting among the ensemble of classifiers

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of genes with count less than 5 reads per sample
Count data normalization using InSyBio Biomarkers tool
Merging of single count files into one file with 471 samples and 60488 features using custom python scripts
Matching of vital status labels from clinical data to samples using python scripts
Statistical analysis using Wilcoxon ranked sum test
Correction of p-values for multiple testing using Benjamini-Hochberg FDR adjustment method
conclusions: The proposed ensemble dimensionality reduction technique employing a multi-objective optimization heuristic algorithm significantly outperformed standard WEKA SVM and Random Forest implementations in predicting the vital status of cutaneous melanoma patients based on RNA-Seq gene expression data.

Prediction accuracy improved as the time window for prognosis shortened, achieving 68.84% accuracy for total follow-up (~30 years), 92.46% for 5-year, 97.80% for 3-year, and 100.00% for 1-year vital status prediction.

The proposed method demonstrated superior handling of imbalanced datasets compared to baseline methods, particularly in the 5-, 3-, and 1-year prediction problems where minority class samples were limited.
The study identified specific gene signatures for prognosis prediction, consisting of 414, 13, 163, and 7 genes for the total, 5-year, 3-year, and 1-year prediction problems, respectively.
A key limitation of the study is the reliance solely on cross-validation for performance metrics without the use of an external independent test set.
The authors acknowledge that single omics data may be insufficient for estimating melanoma prognosis and that future work should integrate multi-omics or multi-modal data.

The ebioMelDB database successfully integrates diverse RNA-Seq gene expression, clinical, and dermoscopy image data, providing a resource to support future research in melanoma diagnosis, treatment response, and prognosis.
important_remarks: The study relies solely on 5-fold cross-validation for performance evaluation and explicitly acknowledges the lack of an external independent test set, which limits the generalizability of the results.
The models were trained exclusively on TCGA-SKCM data (n=471), creating a potential population bias as the cohort is not externally validated on other melanoma datasets.

The paper identifies a significant limitation in using single omics (RNA-Seq) data for prognosis, noting that multi-omics or multi-modal integration is necessary for comprehensive biological description.

The proposed method demonstrates superior handling of class imbalance compared to baseline WEKA implementations, particularly in shorter time windows (5-, 3-, and 1-year) where minority class samples are limited (e.g., 27 samples for 1-year prediction).

Performance metrics improve significantly as the prediction time window shortens (from 68.84% accuracy for ~30-year follow-up to 100.00% for 1-year), which may reflect the difficulty of long-term survival prediction rather than model superiority alone.

The gene signatures identified are small (414, 13, 163, and 7 genes), but the authors note that further optimization of the multi-objective goals could potentially yield even smaller signatures without performance drop.
